learning_objectives: Distinguish between leadership and management roles
Identify key leadership styles and their situational applicability
Assess personal leadership strengths and development areas
readings: Northouse, Leadership: Theory and Practice, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Management ensures things are done right; leadership ensures the right things are done. Project managers need both skills.
key_concepts: Management: planning, organizing, staffing, directing, controlling
Leadership: vision, motivation, influence, empowerment, inspiration
Leadership styles: transformational, transactional, servant, situational
Situational leadership: adapting style to team maturity (directing, coaching, supporting, delegating)
Emotional intelligence: self-awareness, self-regulation, motivation, empathy, social skills
lecture_notes.demonstration: Leadership style self-assessment using a validated instrument and group discussion of results

review.value: The leader serves the team by removing obstacles, providing resources, and creating an environment for success
Match style to follower readiness: direct new team members, coach developing ones, support competent ones, delegate to experts

class_schedule.topic: Team Development and the Tuckman Model
learning_objectives: Apply Tuckman's stages of group development to project teams
Implement team-building activities appropriate to each stage
Develop a team charter establishing norms and working agreements
readings: Tuckman, Developmental Sequence in Small Groups (1965)
Lencioni, The Five Dysfunctions of a Team, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: High-performing teams do not happen by accident. They go through predictable stages, and the leader's role changes at each stage.
key_concepts: Forming: politeness, orientation, dependency on leader; leader directs
Storming: conflict, competition, resistance; leader coaches and mediates
Norming: cohesion, shared standards, trust building; leader supports
Performing: high productivity, autonomy, self-management; leader delegates
Adjourning: disbanding, recognition, knowledge transfer; leader celebrates
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a team charter for a new cross-functional project team including ground rules, decision-making process, and conflict resolution protocol

review.value: Conflict is necessary and healthy; avoiding it keeps the team in forming; the leader must facilitate productive disagreement
Establishes shared expectations for communication, decision-making, meeting behavior, and conflict resolution

learning_objectives: Apply Maslow, Herzberg, and McClelland's motivation theories to project teams
Design intrinsic motivation strategies for knowledge workers
readings: Pink, Drive: The Surprising Truth About What Motivates Us, Ch. 1-4
Herzberg, One More Time: How Do You Motivate Employees?

lecture_notes.introduction: Motivation drives performance. Understanding what motivates team members allows the PM to create conditions for engagement and productivity.
key_concepts: Maslow's hierarchy: physiological, safety, belonging, esteem, self-actualization
Herzberg's two-factor theory: hygiene factors prevent dissatisfaction; motivators drive satisfaction
McClelland's needs: achievement, affiliation, power; different people prioritize differently
Intrinsic motivation (Pink): autonomy, mastery, purpose
Recognition: timely, specific, public; the most accessible motivational tool for PMs
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a motivation strategy for a project team using Pink's autonomy-mastery-purpose framework

review.value: Fixing hygiene factors (salary, conditions) removes dissatisfaction but does not create motivation; motivators (achievement, recognition, growth) drive engagement
For knowledge work, autonomy (control over how), mastery (getting better), and purpose (why it matters) are more powerful than external rewards

learning_objectives: Apply effective delegation principles to project work
readings: Blanchard, The One Minute Manager, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Delegation is not just assigning tasks; it is transferring both authority and responsibility while maintaining accountability.
key_concepts: Delegation levels: tell, sell, consult, agree, advise, inquire, delegate
What to delegate: routine tasks, developmental assignments, tasks others can do better
What not to delegate: performance reviews, confidential matters, crisis management
Decision-making models: autocratic, consultative, consensus, delegation
RACI matrix: Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a RACI matrix for a cross-functional project and identifying delegation opportunities

review.value: Match delegation level to the team member's competence and commitment; gradually increase autonomy as trust builds
Only one person is Accountable per task; multiple can be Responsible, Consulted, or Informed

learning_objectives: Apply coaching techniques to develop team member skills
readings: Stone & Heen, Thanks for the Feedback, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Regular feedback accelerates growth. Waiting for annual reviews to address performance is too late for project timelines.
key_concepts: SBI model: Situation-Behavior-Impact for feedback delivery
Coaching with GROW: Goal, Reality, Options, Will/Way forward
Feedforward: suggestions for future behavior rather than critique of past behavior
Addressing underperformance: document, discuss, develop plan, follow up
Creating psychological safety: team members must feel safe to take risks and raise concerns
lecture_notes.demonstration: Role-playing difficult feedback conversations using SBI model and GROW coaching

review.value: In this Situation, I observed this Behavior, and the Impact was... keeps feedback specific, observable, and non-judgmental
Team members who feel safe to admit mistakes and ask questions perform better and innovate more

learning_objectives: Lead virtual and hybrid project teams effectively
Navigate cultural dimensions that affect team dynamics
Build trust and cohesion in distributed environments
readings: Lepsinger & DeRosa, Virtual Team Success, Ch. 2-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Virtual and cross-cultural teams are now the norm. Leading them requires intentional effort to build what co-located teams develop naturally.
key_concepts: Virtual team challenges: isolation, time zones, communication gaps, trust deficit
Building virtual trust: reliability, competence, personal connection, virtual social time
Hofstede's cultural dimensions: power distance, individualism, uncertainty avoidance
High-context vs. low-context communication in project teams
Inclusive leadership: ensuring all voices are heard regardless of location or culture
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a team onboarding plan for a globally distributed project team across 3 countries

review.value: In virtual teams, trust is built through consistent follow-through on commitments, not through proximity
High power distance cultures expect hierarchy and deference; low power distance cultures expect equality and open challenge

quizzes.topic: Leadership Styles, Team Development, and Motivation

questions.question: A new project team has been together for 3 weeks. Team members are starting to disagree about coding standards, meeting frequency, and who makes technical decisions. What Tuckman stage is the team in, and what specific leadership actions should the PM take?
questions.answer: The team is in the Storming stage. The PM should: (1) normalize the conflict by explaining it is a healthy part of team development, (2) facilitate discussions to resolve the specific disagreements (create a coding standard agreement, set meeting norms, define a decision-making process), (3) use a coaching leadership style to guide the team through disagreements rather than dictating solutions, (4) ensure conflict remains task-focused and does not become personal, (5) use the team charter as a tool to document agreed-upon norms.

rubric.key: Provides specific and appropriate leadership actions
Shows understanding that storming is productive when managed

questions.question: Using Herzberg's two-factor theory, explain why giving a team member a raise may not increase their motivation, and recommend what the PM should do instead.
questions.answer: According to Herzberg, salary is a hygiene factor. Improving it prevents dissatisfaction but does not create motivation. If the team member's salary was already adequate, a raise removes no dissatisfaction and adds no motivation. Instead, the PM should focus on motivators: give the team member more challenging work (achievement), publicly recognize their contributions (recognition), provide opportunities to learn new skills (growth), give them ownership of a meaningful component (responsibility), or involve them in decisions that affect their work (autonomy).

rubric.key: Correctly identifies salary as hygiene factor
Explains why hygiene factors don't create motivation

quizzes.topic: Delegation, Performance Management, and Virtual Teams

questions.question: A PM has a team of 8, including a senior architect (expert, self-motivated) and a junior developer (eager but inexperienced). Using situational leadership, what delegation level and leadership style should the PM use for each, and why?
questions.answer: Senior architect: use a Delegating style (high competence, high commitment). Give them the goal and let them determine the approach. Delegate authority for technical decisions and check in periodically rather than directing. Junior developer: use a Directing/Coaching style (low competence, high commitment). Provide clear instructions, pair them with a mentor, set frequent check-ins, and gradually increase autonomy as they demonstrate competence. Using the same style for both would either micromanage the expert (causing frustration) or overwhelm the junior (causing failure).

rubric.key: Correct style for senior architect with justification
Correct style for junior developer with justification

questions.question: You need to give feedback to a team member who consistently misses deadlines but produces excellent quality work. Write the feedback using the SBI model.
questions.answer: Situation: 'In the last three sprints, specifically for the authentication module, the API integration, and the reporting feature...' Behavior: '...each deliverable was submitted 2-3 days past the agreed deadline, though the quality of the code was consistently excellent with zero defects...' Impact: '...the late deliveries caused downstream teams to miss their integration windows, resulting in a 5-day delay to the release schedule, and the QA team had to work overtime to compress their testing cycle.' Then transition to forward-looking: 'I'd like to discuss what's causing the timeline issues and how we can adjust estimates or scope to maintain your quality standard while meeting schedule commitments.'

rubric.key: Impact stated in measurable project terms

major_assignments.title: Team Leadership Plan and Simulation
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive team leadership plan for a project team of 10-15 members, including team charter, motivation strategy, RACI matrix, and a plan for addressing common team challenges.
objectives: Create a team charter with working agreements and norms
Design a motivation strategy using multiple theoretical frameworks
Build a RACI matrix for a cross-functional project
Develop plans for addressing three specific team leadership challenges
deliverables: Motivation strategy paper applying Herzberg and Pink frameworks (3-4 pages)
RACI matrix for at least 20 project tasks across 5 roles
Three leadership challenge scenarios with detailed response plans (1 page each)

criteria: Charter covers communication norms, decision-making, and conflict resolution
Ground rules are specific and actionable
Correctly applies Herzberg and Pink frameworks
Strategies are specific to the team context, not generic
Addresses both individual and team motivation
RACI properly constructed with one Accountable per task
Response plans reference relevant leadership theories
Plans include specific actions, not just general principles

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on leadership theory, motivation, and team management

leadership_project.description: Team leadership plan and simulation assignment

midterm_examination.description: Leadership styles, team development, and motivation theories

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all leadership and team management topics

class_participation.description: Active participation in role-plays, feedback exercises, and leadership discussions

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all role-play and team-building exercises. More than 3 absences result in a half-letter grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Role-play exercises are collaborative. The leadership plan assignment is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Properly cite all leadership theories and frameworks referenced.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to video conferencing tools for virtual team exercises. No specialized software required.
